text: **Shadow-mode the subscription planner** — lowest friction, highest signal, because the pure function already exists. Model `subscribed_projects` / `identities` / `session_channels` as inputs, add a resource planner keyed by the existing deterministic subscription IDs (structure it on `crates/trellis-examples/src/protocol_subscription.rs`, which is this shape verbatim), feed it the same input writes, and **compare its plan to what `ensure_subscription` actually did** on live traffic with the bespoke path authoritative. The diff is predictable from the report: every leave/switch will show Trellis emitting a `close` the live path never issues — and `why_resource_command` on those closes is the first real receipt the system has ever produced. Then repeat on the 30315 node, then the hook snapshot. Leave `rpc_session_start` for last. [...] Assistant: Both repos are clear. Trellis is a **deterministic reconciliation engine** — state in, effect plans + receipts out, with an explicit design goal of being "legible to tooling and AI agents that need to reason about why a system did what it did, without parsing logs." tenex-edge is a Rust/Nostr daemon that **derives live resources** (relay subscriptions, 30315 status events, presence, hook context) from changing state. That's a promising surface. Let me dig into the real tenex-edge code with parallel Sonnet agents.
